skill.name: azure-ai-contentsafety-java
skill.description: Build Java applications that use Azure AI Content Safety for text moderation, image moderation, blocklist management, and harm severity analysis.
skill.summary: Create Java moderation workflows with Azure AI Content Safety.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for shell execution, weak cryptography, and the critical combined heuristic are false positives from Markdown code fences and moderation terminology. The skill is documentation for Azure AI Content Safety Java usage, with low residual risk from examples that read API credentials from environment variables and call configured service endpoints.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Credential Examples
low_findings.description: The documentation shows reading CONTENT_SAFETY_ENDPOINT and CONTENT_SAFETY_KEY from environment variables. This is an expected Azure SDK pattern, but users must protect these secrets and avoid logging them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents environment variable access for Azure credentials. The usage is legitimate and no exfiltration path is present in the skill.

low_findings.title: Expected Outbound Azure Service Usage
low_findings.description: The examples include a placeholder image URL and an Azure endpoint placeholder. Network access is part of the intended Content Safety SDK workflow and does not indicate hidden data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible placeholders used for documented SDK calls. No hardcoded malicious destination or secret transmission beyond the configured Azure service is shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer matched triple backticks that delimit Markdown examples, not Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file contains Java, XML, and bash snippets for documentation only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are Markdown code fence delimiters around static examples. No instruction tells the assistant to execute those snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Moderation Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to words such as harm, hate, blocklist, and error text in moderation examples. No cryptographic algorithm or hash implementation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is about content moderation categories and blocklist descriptions. There is no crypto API, cipher selection, digest logic, or password handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Critical Combined Heuristic
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical heuristic combines code fences, URL placeholders, and credential configuration examples. The skill contains no hidden script, install hook, prompt injection, or unauthorized network destination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation-only examples for a legitimate Azure SDK. No evidence found of malicious intent, obfuscation, or data exfiltration behavior.

content.user_title: Build Java Content Moderation with Azure AI
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable examples for text and image moderation with Azure AI Content Safety. This skill gives focused SDK patterns for clients, harm analysis, blocklists, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure AI Content Safety Java SDK.
Demonstrates API key and DefaultAzureCredential client creation patterns.
Explains text harm category analysis with configurable severity output.
Shows image analysis from local bytes and URL-based image data.
Covers creating, listing, updating, and deleting text blocklists.
Includes basic HttpResponseException handling for Content Safety calls.
limitations: Uses SDK examples and does not provide a complete production application.
Requires an existing Azure AI Content Safety resource and valid credentials.
Does not define policy decisions for every industry or jurisdiction.
Uses a beta SDK version in the documented dependency example.

use_cases.title: Add moderation to a Java service
use_cases.description: Backend developers can wire Azure text and image safety checks into request processing or review queues.

use_cases.title: Manage custom blocklists
use_cases.description: Trust and safety teams can maintain terms that require stricter handling than general harm classifiers.

use_cases.title: Prototype safety thresholds
use_cases.description: Product teams can compare severity levels before selecting blocking, warning, or escalation rules.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic text moderation call
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a Java service
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure AI Content Safety Java client and analyze one text string for harm categories.

prompt_templates.title: Add selected harm categories
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me analyze only hate and violence categories in Java, then explain how to read the returned severity values.

prompt_templates.title: Design blocklist operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Java workflow for creating a text blocklist, adding items, checking matches, and removing outdated items.

prompt_templates.title: Review a moderation integration plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Java moderation design for credentials, thresholds, retries, blocklist delay, logging, and user appeal handling.

output_examples.input: Analyze a customer comment for safety categories.
output_examples.output: A concise plan describing client setup, the text analysis request, severity interpretation, and a suggested threshold decision.

output_examples.input: Add image moderation to an upload flow.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step outline for reading image content, sending it to Content Safety, and routing high-severity results for review.
A workflow covering blocklist creation, item updates, match checks, propagation delay, and cleanup of obsolete entries.

best_practices: Store Azure credentials in a managed secret store or protected environment variables.
Request only the harm categories needed for the user workflow.
Treat severity thresholds as product policy and review them with safety stakeholders.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode API keys, endpoints, or tenant-specific values in source files.
Do not assume blocklist changes apply instantly across all moderation calls.
Do not log raw user content or moderation secrets without a retention policy.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure calls by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation examples for Java applications that call Azure AI Content Safety.

faq.question: Which authentication methods are shown?
faq.answer: It shows API key authentication and DefaultAzureCredential client creation patterns.

faq.question: Can it moderate images and text?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover text analysis, image byte analysis, and image URL analysis.

faq.question: Does it include blocklist management?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers creating blocklists, adding items, listing entries, removing items, and deleting blocklists.

faq.question: Is this a full compliance solution?
faq.answer: No. It supports implementation work, but policy, compliance, review, and appeals need separate design.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk level low?
faq.answer: The static alerts are mostly false positives, but the examples still involve credentials and outbound Azure service calls.
